text: Sarah lived a hundred twenty-seven years — that was the length of her life.
Sarah died at Kiriath-arba, that is Hebron, in Canaan, and Abraham came to mourn her and weep.
Abraham rose from beside his dead wife and spoke to the Hittites.
He said he was a foreigner living among them and asked for a burial site so he could bury his dead.
The Hittites answered Abraham.
They called him a mighty prince and told him to bury his dead in the choicest of their tombs, none would refuse him.
Abraham rose and bowed to the people of the land, the Hittites.
He asked them to persuade Ephron son of Zohar to sell him the cave at the end of his field.
He asked for the cave of Machpelah at full price, as a permanent burial site.
Ephron the Hittite was sitting among his people and answered Abraham within earshot of all who came to the gate.
Ephron offered to give Abraham both the field and the cave as a gift, before witnesses.
Abraham bowed again before the people of the land.
He insisted on paying the price of the field and asked Ephron to accept it so he could bury his dead there.
Ephron answered Abraham.
He said the land was worth four hundred shekels of silver, but asked what that was between the two of them, and told him to bury his dead.
Abraham weighed out four hundred shekels of silver, the current commercial standard, as Ephron had named it before the Hittites.
So Ephron's field at Machpelah near Mamre, with its cave and every tree within its borders, became legally Abraham's.
It was deeded to Abraham as his possession before all the Hittites who came to the city gate.
After this, Abraham buried his wife Sarah in the cave of the field of Machpelah near Mamre, that is Hebron, in Canaan.
So the field and its cave passed from the Hittites to Abraham as a burial site.
